title: Anesthesia for Trauma and Burns MCQ – Chapter 35
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: Why is management of trauma patients uniquely challenging?

options.A: Inadequate medical history, not fasting, intoxication, multiple injuries, shock and difficult airway

questions.explanation: Management of trauma patients poses unique challenges due to inadequate medical history, most patients are not fasting, may be intoxicated, multiple injuries make anesthesia complex, airway may be difficult due to facial/neck injuries, and presence of shock.

questions.question: Why is adequate medical history often unavailable in trauma patients?

options.D: Attendant may not be present and patient may not be fit enough to give history

questions.explanation: Inadequate medical history: Attendant may not be present and patient may not be fit enough to give his/her medical history.

questions.question: What is the minimum systolic blood pressure recommended before induction in trauma patients?
questions.explanation: A systolic blood pressure of at least 80-90 mm Hg is recommended before induction.

questions.question: When may damage control surgery warrant induction at very low blood pressure?

options.A: When surgery is the only method to stop bleeding

questions.explanation: Sometimes the situation like damage control surgery (surgery is the only method to stop bleeding) warrants the induction at very low or even nonrecordable blood pressure.

questions.question: What major injuries must be ruled out in trauma patients?

options.B: Chest injury (pneumo/hemothorax), head injury, spine injuries, abdominal and pelvic injuries

questions.explanation: It is very important to rule out major injuries especially chest injury like pneumo/hemothorax, head injury, spine injuries, and abdominal/pelvic injuries.

questions.question: Why is head injury significant for choice of anesthesia technique?

options.B: Central neuraxial blocks may be absolutely contraindicated with elevated intracranial tension (ICT)

questions.explanation: Head injury—presence of head injury can alter the anesthesia technique. For example, central neuraxial blocks may be absolutely contraindicated with elevated intracranial tension (ICT).

questions.question: What devastating complications can improper movements of spine injury patients cause?
questions.explanation: Spine injuries: Improper movements can produce devastating complications, including quadriplegia, paraplegia or even death.

questions.question: If fasting status cannot be obtained in trauma, what premedication should be given?
questions.explanation: If fasting status cannot be obtained, pre-medicate the patient with metoclopramide and ranitidine/sodium citrate.

questions.question: Why is continuous blood pressure by invasive monitoring recommended in trauma?

options.A: Although difficult to cannulate artery in low output state, every effort should be made as trauma patients are prone to hypotension

questions.explanation: Although it may be difficult to cannulate artery in low output state but still every effort should be made to measure continuous blood pressure by invasive monitoring.

questions.question: Why is temperature monitoring essential in trauma patients?

options.C: Because trauma patients are very prone to hypothermia

questions.explanation: As trauma patients are very prone for hypothermia, temperature monitoring becomes must.

questions.question: How should a trauma patient always be considered regarding stomach contents?

options.B: As having a full stomach until proved otherwise, managed like rapid sequence induction

questions.explanation: A trauma patient should always be considered full stomach until proved otherwise and should be managed like rapid sequence induction.

questions.question: What is the choice of anesthesia for majority of trauma surgeries?

options.C: General anesthesia (except for limb saving surgeries which may be done under regional)

questions.explanation: Except for limb saving surgeries which may be performed in regional anesthesia, majority of the trauma surgeries are done in general anesthesia.

questions.question: Why is selection of induction agent difficult in trauma patients?

options.C: IV induction agents may produce profound hypotension or even cardiac arrest in shock patients

questions.explanation: Selection of the induction agent is difficult in trauma patients. IV induction agents may produce profound hypotension (or even cardiac arrest) in shock patients.

questions.question: How is the dose requirement of IV induction agents in hypotensive patients?
questions.explanation: Requirement of doses is drastically reduced in hypotensive patients.

questions.question: Which agents are often selected for induction in trauma patients and why?

options.A: Etomidate (cardiovascular stability) and ketamine (sympathetic system stimulating property)

questions.explanation: Etomidate (because of its cardiovascular stability) and ketamine (because of its sympathetic system stimulating property) are the agents often selected for induction.

questions.question: Why can etomidate still cause hypotension in catecholamine-depleted shock patients?
questions.explanation: Etomidate can produce hypotension in shock patients who are already catecholamine depleted; etomidate by causing adrenocortical suppression.

questions.question: Why can ketamine cause hypotension in catecholamine-depleted patients?

options.B: It causes direct myocardial depression in the absence of catecholamine

questions.explanation: Ketamine can produce hypotension in shock patients who are already catecholamine depleted; ketamine by causing direct myocardial depression in the absence of catecholamine.

questions.question: What should be assumed about ALL trauma patients regarding cervical spine?

options.C: All should be assumed to have cervical spine injury until proved otherwise

questions.explanation: All trauma patients should be assumed to be having cervical spine injury until proved otherwise.

questions.question: How should trauma patients with uncleared cervical spine be intubated?
questions.explanation: If cervical spine injury is not cleared they should be intubated with in-line manual stabilization.

questions.question: When should nasal route NOT be acquired for intubation in trauma?

options.B: Until basal skull fracture is ruled out

questions.explanation: Nasal route should not be acquired for intubation until basal skull fracture is ruled out.

questions.question: What equipment must be ready for difficult airway in trauma including face/neck trauma?

options.C: All equipment for difficult airway including emergency cricothyrotomy

questions.explanation: All equipment for difficult airway (including emergency cricothyrotomy) must be ready hand available because intubating a patient with face and neck trauma can become a big challenge.

questions.question: Through which route can patients with maxillary fractures most often be easily intubated?
questions.explanation: Patients with maxillary fractures should not be intubated nasally; however most often can be easily intubated through oral route.

questions.question: Why are patients with mandibular fracture often intubated nasally?

options.C: Trismus prevents oral intubation and maxillofacial surgeons do dental wiring to close mouth after surgery

questions.explanation: Patients with mandibular fracture are often intubated nasally due to two reasons: trismus prevents oral intubation, and most often maxillofacial surgeons do the dental wiring to close the mouth after mandibular surgery.

questions.question: What may patients with both mandibular and maxillary fracture require?
questions.explanation: Patients with both mandibular and maxillary fracture may require tracheostomy.

questions.question: What is the best solution for pharyngeal and laryngeal trauma where intubation can worsen the injury?
questions.explanation: Intubation in pharyngeal and laryngeal trauma can worsen the injury or even lead to complete loss of airway. A low tracheostomy is the best solution in most of the cases.

questions.question: Is application of cricoid pressure contraindicated in any trauma situation?

options.C: Yes, it is contraindicated in laryngeal trauma

questions.explanation: Application of cricoid pressure is also contraindicated in laryngeal trauma.

questions.question: Why must a chest tube be inserted before intubation if there is pneumothorax?

options.A: Because positive pressure ventilation can convert a small pneumothorax into tension pneumothorax

questions.explanation: Chest tube must be inserted before intubation if there is pneumothorax because positive pressure ventilation can convert a small pneumothorax into tension pneumothorax.

questions.question: Which inhalational agent is most preferred for maintenance in trauma patients?
questions.explanation: Desflurane is often the most preferred inhalational agent because of its ability to maintain blood pressure by causing sympathetic stimulation and least metabolism.

questions.question: Why may patients in shock exhibit extreme sensitivity to inhalational agents?

options.A: Like IV agents, patients in shock exhibit extreme sensitivity to inhalational agents

questions.explanation: Like IV agents patients in shock may exhibit extreme sensitivity to inhalational agents.

questions.question: Despite causing hyperkalemia in crush injury, why does succinylcholine remain the muscle relaxant of choice in trauma?

options.A: Because it remains the choice for intubation in difficult airway and high risk of aspiration - consequences of failed intubation and aspiration can be devastating

questions.explanation: In spite of succinylcholine causing hyperkalemia in crush injury and increasing intraocular and intracranial pressure, it remains the muscle relaxant of choice for intubation in trauma patients with difficult airway and high risk of aspiration.

questions.question: Which non-depolarizing muscle relaxants are preferred in trauma?

options.C: Atracurium and Cis-atracurium (due to their independency on hepatic and renal functions)

questions.explanation: Atracurium and Cis-atracurium are preferred non depolarizers because of their independency on hepatic and renal functions.

questions.question: What type of IV access should be secured in trauma patients?

options.D: Large bore cannulas; if peripheral access not possible then central access (most often subclavian) or intraosseous access

questions.explanation: Secure IV lines with large bore cannulas. If peripheral access is not possible, then obtain central access (most often subclavian) or intraosseous access (if central access is also not possible).

questions.question: How should hypothermia be prevented in trauma patients?

options.C: OT temperature should be maintained and fluids/blood should be warmed to room temperature before infusion

questions.explanation: As trauma patients are very prone for hypothermia OT temperature should be maintained and fluids/blood should be warmed to room temperature before infusion.

questions.question: Why is extubation tricky in trauma patients?

options.B: Deep extubation can cause aspiration while light extubation can increase intraocular/intracranial pressure and restart hemorrhage

questions.explanation: Extubation in trauma patients is a tricky exercise. Deep extubation can cause aspiration while extubation under light anesthesia can increase intraocular, intracranial pressure and restart the hemorrhage by increasing blood pressure.

questions.question: Is it uncommon to see trauma patients getting electively ventilated postoperatively?
questions.explanation: It is not uncommon to see trauma patients getting electively ventilated in postoperative period.

questions.question: What is the most common immediate cause of death if burns occur in closed space?
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning is the most common immediate cause of death if burns occur in closed space.

questions.question: What is the immediate treatment for carbon monoxide poisoning in burns?

options.B: Oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen (in severe cases)

questions.explanation: Immediate treatment with oxygen or hyperbaric oxygen (in severe cases) may be required.

questions.question: What formula is used for fluid replacement in burn patients?
questions.explanation: Fluid should be given by the standard Parkland formula which recommends fluid replacement at a rate of 4 mL/kg/% burn.

questions.question: How should the Parkland formula fluid be distributed?

options.D: 50% in first 8 hours and next 50% in next 16 hours

questions.explanation: 50% of this fluid is given in first 8 hours and next 50% in next 16 hours.

questions.question: What is the current recommendation for fluid management in burns?

options.C: Start fluid by Parkland formula but immediately start tapering once urine output becomes >0.5-1 mL/kg/hr

questions.explanation: Current recommendation is to start fluid by Parkland formula but immediately start tapering once urine output becomes >0.5-1 mL/kg/hr.

questions.question: What is the fluid of choice in burn resuscitation?
questions.explanation: Ringer lactate is the fluid of choice.

questions.question: Can colloids be given in acute phase of burns?

options.A: Yes, if necessary can be given in acute phase (contrary to previous recommendation of not giving colloid for first 48 hours)

questions.explanation: Colloids if necessary can be given in acute phase (contrary to previous recommendation of not giving colloid for first 48 hours).

questions.question: What cardiac complications can electrical burns present with?

options.B: Cardiac arrhythmias or even cardiac arrest

questions.explanation: Patients with electrical burns can present with cardiac arrhythmias or even cardiac arrest.

questions.question: Why is intraoperative ECG monitoring mandatory in electrical burns?

options.D: Because there are high chances of arrhythmias

questions.explanation: Intraoperative monitoring of ECG is mandatory in electric burns as there are high chances of arrhythmias.

questions.question: What is the usual surgery done in burn patients?

options.B: Release of post-burn contractures and split skin grafts

questions.explanation: Usual surgeries done in burn patients are release of post-burn contractures and split skin grafts.

questions.question: Why is succinylcholine contraindicated in burn patients and for how long?

options.A: For one year due to presence of extrajunctional receptors causing hyperkalemia

questions.explanation: Presence of extrajunctional receptors contraindicates the use of succinylcholine for one year and decreases the sensitivity (increase dose requirement) of nondepolarizers.

questions.question: Why may the dose of IV induction agent be slightly higher in burn patients?

options.C: Due to large volume of distribution (hyperdynamic circulation) and increased metabolism

questions.explanation: Dose of intravenous induction agent may be slightly higher because of the large volume of distribution (hyperdynamic circulation) and increased metabolism.

questions.question: Why may airway be difficult in burn patients?

options.D: Due to contracture of face and neck

questions.explanation: Airway may be difficult due to contracture of face and neck. Awake intubation under fiberoptic bronchoscopy may be required.

questions.question: If fiberoptic intubation is not possible in burn contracture patients, what alternative is used?

options.C: Release contracture under IV ketamine and then intubation is done

questions.explanation: If that is not possible then contracture is released under intravenous ketamine and then intubation is done.

questions.question: What should be evaluated in patients with electrical burns before giving regional anesthesia?

options.A: Neurological deficits (as electrical burns can have neurological injuries)

questions.explanation: Patients with electrical burn can have neurological injuries. They should be evaluated for neurological deficits before giving regional anesthesia.
